data.title: Arthur J. Gallagher slips after BMO downgrades on growth concerns
data.description:
** Shares of insurance broker Arthur J. Gallagher down 2.88% at $257.22

** BMO downgrades AJG to "market perform" from "outperform"

** Brokerage also cuts PT to $275 from $289, which is still a 3.8% upside to the stock's last close

** Brokerage cites concerns around profit margins and organic growth visibility

** 4Q earnings are expected to be bumpy, with most strength concentrated in 1Q’26 due to seasonality in reinsurance and employee benefits segments, brokerage says

** 13 of 23 brokerages rate the stock "buy" or higher, 9 "hold" and 1 "sell"; median PT is $285 - LSEG

** Including session's move, AJG down 10% in the last 12 months

data.title: Central Bank Chiefs Back Fed Chair Jerome Powell In Fight With Trump
data.description:
CANBERA (dpa-AFX) - Heads of several central banks across the world expressed their support for Fed Chair Jerome Powell in his fight against the Trump administration that is threatening him with criminal charges, in an assault on central bank independence.

'We stand in full solidarity with the Federal Reserve System and its Chair Jerome H. Powell,' a group of central bank chief including the European Central Bank President Christine Lagarde and Bank of England Governor Andrew Bailey said in a joint statement on Tuesday.

'The independence of central banks is a cornerstone of price, financial and economic stability in the interest of the citizens that we serve,' the central bankers said. 'It is therefore critical to preserve that independence, with full respect for the rule of law and democratic accountability.'

'Chair Powell has served with integrity, focused on his mandate and an unwavering commitment to the public interest,' they said. 'To us, he is a respected colleague who is held in the highest regard by all who have worked with him.'

Other central bank governors who were part of the move to support Powell included those of Sweden and Denmark, Erik Thedéen and Christian Kettel Thomsen. They were joined by the Swiss National Bank Board Chairman Martine Schlegel, the Reserve Bank of Australia Governor Michele Bullock, Bank of Canada Chief Tiff Macklem, Bank of Korea head Chang Yong Rhee and Brazil's central bank chief Garbriel Galipolo.

The Chair of the Board of Directors of the Bank for International Settlements and the General Manager of the BIS François Villeroy de Galhau and Pablo Hernández de Cos also were involved.

The statement was issued after Powell revealed on Sunday that the U.S. central bank has been served subpoenas by the Department of Justice that threaten criminal charges, raising concerns that the Trump administration is trying to undermine the institution's independence.

Federal prosecutors have launched a criminal investigation of Powell over his June testimony to Congress regarding the $2.5 billion renovation project for three buildings, including the Eccles building that serves a the headquarters of the Fed.

Concerns over the central bank independence, which is considered sacred in the financial world, rattled markets as investors latched onto the 'Sell America' sentiment. Gold, a safe haven, hit a new record as investors scurried for cover.

Former Fed Chairs Janet Yellen, Ben Bernanke and Alan Greenspan, as well as former Treasury chiefs Timothy Geithner, Jacob Lew, Hank Paulson and Robert Rubin also hit out against the DOJ subpoenas against Powell on Monday. They expressed concern regarding the Trump administration's efforts to undermine the Fed's independence and its impact on the U.S. economy.

The action against Powell, whose term ends in May, is also seen as a message from the Trump administration to whomever succeeds him to toe the line.

Trump has been highly vocal in criticizing the Fed as he seeks a drastic lowering of interest rates and even threatened to remove Powell as well as sought a say in interest rate decisions. Trump's budget watchdog chief Russel Vought has also accused Powell of gross mismanagement of the Fed.

data.title: Dow Slips; Core Inflation Is Softer Than Forecast — WSJ
data.description:
By Caitlin McCabe

Investors got a double whammy of results and inflation data on Tuesday morning, with JPMorgan Chase kicking off bank earnings and the consumer-price index for December rolling in.

Headline prices rose by 2.7% from a year earlier, as expected and in line with November's rate. Core CPI, which excludes food and energy prices, also held at the prior month's rate, rather than accelerating as forecast.

The Dow industrials lagged behind other indexes, falling about 0.6%, weighed down by declines in JPMorgan and Visa. JPMorgan posted a 7% drop in quarterly profit on revenue that missed forecasts. Treasury yields slipped.

Investor anxiety about the investigation into Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell appeared to abate slightly, with the dollar partially unwinding Monday's decline. Global central bankers and JPMorgan Chief Executive Jamie Dimon defended Powell and the Fed's independence.

Oil prices extended gains, rising more than 2%. President Trump encouraged Iranians to keep protesting and said "help is on its way." On Monday he moved to impose a 25% tariff on countries doing business with Iran.

In other markets:

Japan's Nikkei 225 surged to a record, while the yen and government bonds fell, on rising expectations of a snap election next month.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Microsoft and Apple Could Join the $4 Trillion Club in 2026
data.description:
Microsoft Corp and Apple Inc sit within striking distance of a $4 trillion market valuation and remain the likeliest candidates to clear that threshold in 2026, analysts say.

Microsoft trades near $475.57 a share, valuing the company at about $3.55 trillion. The software and cloud provider benefits from strong enterprise demand for artificial intelligence tools, including its Copilot model and a strategic relationship with OpenAI, and continued growth in Azure.

Revenue rose from $211.9 billion in 2023 to roughly $293.8 billion for the 12 months ended Sept. 30, 2025, and the average Wall Street 12-month target implies a roughly 32% upside to about $631.36.

Apple's market capitalization stands near $3.85 trillion, meaning a modest share-price gain could push it past $4 trillion. The company recently agreed to use Google AI as a foundation for its Apple Foundation Models, a move analysts view as a catalyst. Wall Street's average 12-month target implies roughly a 15% gain.

Both firms face risks tied to the pace of AI adoption and potential regulatory or cost pressures, including proposals to shift data-center electricity costs to operators. Recent share pullbacks since December temper near-term expectations but leave longer-term upside intact, analysts say.

data.title: Gasoline refining margins fall
data.description:
Northwest European gasoline refinery profit margins fell by 34 cents on Tuesday to $9.22 a barrel.

  • About 11,000 metric tons of Eurobob E5 gasoline traded on barges in the Argus window as Equinor, ExxonMobil and Trafigura sold to BP, Varo and TotalEnergies.

  • No Eurobob E10 gasoline barges traded.

  • Sinochem's Quanzhou Petrochemical said on Tuesday that it is close to completing maintenance at its refining-chemical complex in Fujian province, southeastern China, and expects to gradually resume operations soon.

  • The Chevron vessel among those reported to have come under drone attack near the Caspian Pipeline Consortium's terminal in the Black Sea on Tuesday was proceeding to a safe port, the U.S. oil major said.

  • Meanwhile, EU-27 and UK gasoline exports to other regions have averaged 508,000 barrels per day in January so far, compared to 773,000 bpd across the full month of December.

data.title: Denmark's Rockwool loses control of Russian factories
data.description:
Danish building materials company Rockwool said on Tuesday that Russia has taken control of the company's four factories in Russia and the company is no longer in control of its assets in the country.

Rockwool added that the factories will be de-consolidated and the net value will be written down.

data.description:
RNS Number : 7423O Saba Capital Management L.P 13 January 2026  

NOT FOR RELEASE, PUBLICATION OR DISTRIBUTION IN WHOLE OR IN PART, DIRECTLY OR INDIRECTLY IN, INTO OR FROM ANY JURISDICTION WHERE TO DO SO WOULD CONSTITUTE A VIOLATION OF THE RELEVANT LAWS OR REGULATIONS OF THAT JURISDICTION

SABA LETTER TO WORKSPACE GROUP PLC

Saba Capital Releases Letter Sent to Workspace Group Plc's Board of Directors

Saba Capital Management, L.P. (together with certain of its affiliates, "Saba" or "we"), which owns approximately 13.5% of the outstanding share capital of Workspace Group plc (WKP:LSE) (the "Company"), today disclosed that it sent a letter to the Company's Board of Directors (the "Board") on 8 January 2026. In the letter, Saba urged the Board to recommend the Company proceed with a managed wind-down, which should include an orderly strategic sale of its assets, systematic repayment of debt and the timely return of capital to shareholders.

A copy of Saba's letter can be found here.

About Saba

Saba Capital Management, L.P. is a global alternative asset management firm that seeks to deliver superior risk-adjusted returns for a diverse group of clients. data.title: Toronto Stocks Flat; Maple Leaf Foods Rises on 2026 Outlook, Dividend Increase
data.description:
By Adriano Marchese

Toronto-listed stocks were flat at midday on Tuesday. Sector performance was mixed, with the main laggards in tech services, communications and transportation stocks. Distribution services, health services and energy were the main gainers, offsetting the declines.

On the building front, the total value of building permits dropped 13.1% in November from the month before to a seasonally adjusted 12.03 billion Canadian dollars, the equivalent of US$8.67 billion, according to Statistics Canada.

Canada's S&P/TSX Composite Index rose 0.1% to 32889.68, and the blue-chip S&P/TSX 60 was flat at 1917.61.

Maple Leaf Foods shares rose 4.2% to C$25.89 after the consumer-packaged-protein company said it expects revenue to grow in 2026, supported by gains in its sustainable meats business and continued investment in core brands and markets. It also said it plans to increase its quarterly dividend.

Other market movers:

Global Atomic shares fell 21% to C$0.75 after the Canadian uranium producer said it has raised another C$25 million in a second private placement, bringing the total raised to C$75 million.

Cineplex shares fell 1.8% to C$10.90 after it said its fourth-quarter revenue fell short of the same period a year earlier despite a strong December box office.

Write to Adriano Marchese at adriano.marchese@wsj.com
